title: Lesson 412
transcript: A disaster has occurred and now you're beginning to restore systems. Which systems are you gonna restore first? This is an important question that you're gonna have to contemplate, right? If you're in the midst of a disaster or something cataclysmic has happened, like your whole primary data center has burned to the ground, you're going to have to make decisions as to which systems you restore first, because you cannot restore absolutely everything at the same time. So how are you going to determine what you're going to restore first? In what order? And so the first thing here is you're obviously gonna restore your most critical systems first. And that makes sense, right? Because what is business continuity management focus on overall? The most critical and essential functions. So obviously you're gonna restore your most critical systems first. but it's not quite that simple. We also have to think about dependencies. So what this means here, right? Is that like, let's say our most critical system is the finance system. Well, maybe the finance system requires some backend database. And the only way to log into the finance system is if Active Directory is up and running. And so now in order to restore the finance system, what we first have to bring up is probably the database and part of the network and maybe Active Directory so we can log in. And so this is where dependency charts come in. So yes, you want to restore your most critical system first. But you also have to think about what dependencies do the most critical systems have. And this is where you can start creating what are known as dependency charts or dependency mappings. I'm going to show you a really simple example here. This can obviously get horrendously complicated in a large, complex organization. But here's the basic idea. Let's say our most critical system is Deskcert.com. Well, destert.com, the website, might require that a load balancer be up and running. And that load balancer might require that there is a cluster of web servers up and running. And those web servers require a back end database. This is what a dependency chart or dependency mapping looks like. You know, this needs that, which needs this, which needs that. And so in order to get Deathcert.com up and running, our most critical system, the first thing we might need to get up is our database. Once the database is up, then we can bring up the web servers. Once those are up, then we can bring up the load balancer. And then destruct.com will be up. So this is often used as part of disaster recovery planning to figure out what systems are you going to restore in what order. Your most critical systems first, but what do they depend on? So you know the answer to this question, right? What systems operations should be moved first to the DR site? So if your primary data center fails, what are you gonna move first to your disaster recovery site? Obviously your most critical systems. What about this question though? Let's say that your primary data center has burned to the ground. You've moved all your most critical systems over to your secondary, your recovery site, and you've now rebuilt your primary data center. And now you're starting to migrate systems from the recovery site back to the newly rebuilt primary site. In this situation, what are you going to move first from your recovery site back to your rebuilt primary site? What are you going to move first? And the answer is your least critical systems. because if you've just rebuilt a whole data center there will be bugs there will be issues and you do not want to discover those issues with your most critical systems because you're just going to cause another major disaster so when you start migrating things back to say the rebuilt primary site what you want to migrate first is your least critical things discover the bugs, find the issues with those least critical things, and build back on up to your most critical things.
